title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear an adventure photographer called Ryan Miller talking about his work capturing images in extreme mountain environments. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear an adventure photographer called Ryan Miller talking about his work capturing images in extreme mountain environments. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: Ryan Miller discusses the technical and physical challenges of photography at high altitudes.
extracts.audio_script:
Hi, I’m Ryan. Most photographers work in studios; my office is usually ten thousand feet above sea level. I specialize in high-altitude adventure photography. My interest in the mountains wasn't something I grew up with; it actually began when I won a [7]photography competition[/7] in a national magazine. Part of the prize was a guided trek in the Himalayas, and as soon as I saw the light hitting those peaks, I knew I couldn't go back to wedding photography.

Working in extreme cold presents massive technical problems. Batteries that usually last all day can die in twenty minutes when the temperature drops below zero. I’ve found that the [8]battery storage[/8] is the most critical part of my preparation. I have to keep them inside my jacket, close to my body heat, until the very second I’m ready to take the shot. If they get cold, the internal chemistry fails, and the camera becomes a very expensive paperweight.

Then there’s the physical toll. You aren't just a photographer; you have to be an elite climber too. Carrying thirty kilos of gear while oxygen levels are low is exhausting. I’ve noticed that [9]mental fatigue[/9] is actually more dangerous than physical tiredness. When your brain is starved of oxygen, you start making simple mistakes—forgetting to check your harness or miscalculating your exposure settings. You have to be incredibly disciplined about your safety routines.

The light at high altitudes is unlike anything on the ground. Because the atmosphere is thinner, the colors are much more intense, but the shadows are also much darker. I found that using a [10]circular polarizer[/10] is essential to manage the glare from the snow. Without it, the camera sensor is simply overwhelmed by the brightness, and you lose all the detail in the landscape. It’s about taming the light so the viewer can see what you see.

Safety is, of course, the priority. We spend weeks studying weather patterns before a shoot. I’ve found that the [11]wind speed[/11] is the factor that most often forces us to cancel a project. It’s not just the danger of being blown off a ridge; the vibration makes it impossible to get a sharp image, even with a tripod. You have to respect the mountain; it doesn't care about your deadline.

One of the most rewarding projects I’ve done involved documenting a remote village in the Andes. I noticed that the [12]solar panels[/12] we brought along were more fascinating to the locals than the cameras themselves. It was a reminder that while I’m there for the art, for them, technology is about survival and improving their daily lives. We ended up leaving much of our power equipment behind as a gift.

Looking forward, I’m worried about the impact of 'over-tourism' on these fragile environments. Social media has made some remote spots so famous that they are being damaged by the sheer number of visitors. I believe that [13]stricter regulations[/13] for permits are necessary to protect these peaks for the next generation. We have a responsibility to leave no trace.

Finally, I’m currently putting together a [14]touring gallery[/14] of my work from the last decade. It’s not just about pretty pictures; I want to highlight how climate change is affecting the glaciers I’ve been shooting for years. I hope it starts a conversation about conservation. Thanks for your time.

dialogue.speaker: ryan
